Cool! Nice approach that can further streamline the CORS UX. 🎉  

Was able to replicate with the following steps:

1. create a CloudFront distribution, as per our [docs](https://docs.localstack.cloud/user-guide/aws/cloudfront/):
```
$ awslocal s3 mb s3://test
$ echo 'Hello World' > /tmp/hello.txt
$ awslocal s3 cp /tmp/hello.txt s3://test/hello.txt --acl public-read

$ domain=$(awslocal cloudfront create-distribution --origin-domain-name test.s3.amazonaws.com | jq -r '.Distribution.DomainName')
$ echo $domain
ce6554a5.cloudfront.localhost.localstack.cloud
```

2. create a simple `index.html` file that connects to the enpoint:
```
<html>
<script>
fetch('http://ce6554a5.cloudfront.localhost.localstack.cloud:4566/hello.txt');
</script>
</html>
```

3. upload the file to a bucket and expose it as an S3 website:
```
awslocal s3 cp /tmp/index.html s3://test/index.html --content-type text/html
awslocal s3api put-bucket-website --bucket test --website-configuration 'ErrorDocument={Key=index.html},IndexDocument={Suffix=index.html}'
```

4. open http://test.s3-website.localhost.localstack.cloud:4566/index.html in the browser, observe the Network console (request to `ce6554a5.cloudfront.localhost.localstack.cloud:4566`)

Result:
* returns a `403` error on current master  🟥 
* returns `200` and expected file content with the changes in this PR  ✅ 

---
To me, this achieves a decent balance between improving convenience (works out of the box without extra configs), while not sacrificing security (requests still only allowed from `*.localhost.localstack.cloud` pages in the browser). 👍 As usual with CORS or security-critical configurations, would be good to get a second pair of eyes on it, though.. 👀 /cc @dfangl @alexrashed @thrau

Very excited for this! I've got thoughts on a few different pieces:

### Asset status

I think the "asset status" idea is interesting, and I could see it working well once we add some additional concepts around asset status, but I don't think a dagster event type is closely analogous enough to a dagster run status for this to make sense right now:
- An event type says "this thing happened"
- A run status says "this run is now in this particular state"

Let me know if there are particular use cases you have in mind that we'd miss out on without the status functionality?

### User-provided functions

Thoughts on combining the trigger_fn and the evaluation function? I.e. instead of this:

```
@asset_status_sensor(
    asset_keys=[AssetKey("asset_c"), AssetKey("asset_d")],
    asset_status=DagsterEventType.ASSET_MATERIALIZATION,
    trigger_fn=lambda x: all(x.values()),
    job=my_job,
)
def asset_c_or_d_sensor(context, _asset_events):
    return RunRequest(run_key=context.cursor)
```

this: 

```
@asset_status_sensor(
    asset_keys=[AssetKey("asset_c"), AssetKey("asset_d")],
    asset_status=DagsterEventType.ASSET_MATERIALIZATION,
    trigger_fn=lambda x: all(x.values()),
    job=my_job,
)
def asset_c_or_d_sensor(context, _asset_events):
    if all(context.events_by_monitored_asset_key.values()):
        return RunRequest(run_key=context.cursor)
```

A few reasons this could be beneficial:
- Supplying functions to other functions make code kind of awkward.  E.g. if the triggering logic is complex, it might not fit into a lambda, which means the triggering code needs to be located in a module-scope block that's separate from the rest of the sensor definition.
- The RunRequests might depend on the same data as the trigger function.
- The decision of whether to trigger might depend on other data inside the context, like the cursor or other events on the instance.

Hey @rjmcginness, i think we need one more round of polishing and then we're good to go.

I took the liberty of correcting the integration tests (which had several problems) and adding the corresponding snapshots.
Just to clarify:
* We typically run newly added integration tests locally first to make sure they don't have any major issues
* `sqs_queue` in the context of a test method is a pytest test fixture which creates a temprary queue, and the `sqs_queue` parameter is simply a string, specifically, the temporary queue's URL. i see now that it can be a bit misleading with the class `SqsQueue`, and that there may be confusion that it's the internal queue object you're being handed.
* The `@pytest.mark.aws_validated` marker indicates that someone has validated this test against AWS, meaning the test was executed against the real remote AWS with an AWS account and the test has passed. This can be done by setting `TEST_TARGET=AWS_CLOUD` and running the tests
* snapshot tests are a bit tricky (which is why i thought i would add them to make things easier), but in combination with `TEST_TARGET=AWS_CLOUD`, you can set `SNAPSHOT_UPDATE=1`, which will execute the test against AWS, and also record the response (see the updated snapshot json file), when you remove `SNAPSHOT_UPDATE=1` again, the test will verify against the recorded AWS response

I did all that. So what's left to do is to fix the error responses according to the snapshots :-) We should see the test results from our CI in about 30 minutes.

This all is great, except maybe for the name. `after_option` implies a temporal sequence, which makes sense to people who know about how it's implemented—that the control flow starts at the root of the tree and walks downward, so "after" means "deep" or "nested".  What this is doing is sharing the option-type among the merged arrays, so maybe "shared"?

How about this very explicit name: `optiontype_outside_record`? This function always creates a record/tuple at some level, so we know which record it's talking about. Including the "type" in "optiontype" disambiguates other possible uses of "option", which can also suggest user choices, nothing to do with a type in which some values can be null. So far, this is my best suggestion.

I'm leaving this open so that you can pick another name, but once you've done that, you can squash-and-merge it. The implementation is great.

A few years later, some review comments (I'm mostly looking at this from the POV of the Signer interface and  #447):

I think we should not expose all of this as a new module -- especially things like `get_keys_on_hsm()`, `get_hsms()` seem like things that should not be public API here

`HSMSigner` on the other hand sounds like a reasonable thing: The API would include 
* key and device ids as constructor arguments (optional if PyCKS supports that)
* PKCS module loaded using environment variable (optional if there are reasonable defaults for some systems)
* PIN as constructor variable

This would be compatible with #447 with a URI like `hsm:device/0/slot/c9` or `hsm:?slot=c9&device=0`, and pin getting requested with `secrets_handler()`
 
The remaining functionality would then be `export_pubkey()`. Interestingly this same issue comes up with KMS keys... Maybe _public key export_ could actually be a method on the Signer as well: not all implementations would support it but some could?

Another thing that comes up with KMS keys as well is the need to prehash the payload. We should add that support somewhere -- figuring out the algorithm from the public key is currently very annoying. Can we have `Key.get_payload_hash_algorithm()`?

Left also some code comments -- they may be obsolete so feel free to disregard

I wonder if at this point, with numpy and matplotlib so commonly available if it's just worth it to put them and jsonpickle into the requirements.txt file -- I used to want to keep the install super minimal for people on embedded systems, but now I wonder how many people are running a Python installation that can't really run them.

I wish that there were a robust solution to the problem I posted at https://stackoverflow.com/questions/53376135/install-by-default-optional-dependencies-in-python-setuptools

1. `--json` オプションの名前を別のものに変更した上で `--help` では表示されないようにしてください。
    `--json` オプションは他のユーザが利用している (あるいは今後利用してしまう) かもしれないためです。`$ oj t --json | jq .` などとして動いていたものが急に動かなくなったとして、`--json` オプションが削除されたのならばそれは「単なる仕様の変更」ということになります。しかし削除されたわけではないのに動かなくなったとすれば、それは「このプルリク #825 のせいでバグが埋め込まれてしまった」ということになってしまいます。
2. 「内部的なテストでしか使われず、もし予期しない入力がされて壊れてもせいぜいテストが落ちるだけ」「`--json` オプションが消えるとこのコードも消える」という性質のコードなので、実装はこれで問題ないと思います。
  一方で、なぜこのような「若干強引な」方法が用いられているかについて、コード中にコメントとして簡単に書いておいてほしいです。この issue やプルリクへのリンクを貼る程度でもかまいません。

> `--json` オプションに関しては消したいとのことなので、`--log-file=path/to.json` の方式もちょっとチャレンジしてみようかと思います。

I think we would want a generic way to load target field values from files on disk in a generic way, akin to the `http_source` object.

```python
target(
  tags=file_source("path/to/my-tags.yaml"),
  description=file_source("path/to/description.txt"),
)
```
Looks good!  I suggested two small changes meant to make it more alluring to prospective users.

I think that what you're trying to achieve is what I'll call generic RPC behavior. IOW, a class that can be mapped to a call on the manager that can do **any** RPC.

Rather than changing an existing class to do more than originally intended, I think it would be better to have a concrete class in rpc.py that can be mapped into a function called `rpc` on the manager, and it takes either a string or an XML tree. This would just be sent with no extra parameters, and the reply would come back as an RPCReply object.

I'm suggesting this because I think that there should only be one way to do things, and I actually think that the `dispatch` call is something I'd rather deprecate if at all possible (this is probably not possible until **after** we declare a 1.0.0 release as I don't want to make any API breaking changes in `0.x.y`).

@Frassle @RobbieMcKinstry thank you for your (very prompt!) feedback!

Based on @Frassle's comment I modified this to no longer set defaults during import etc. Now we'll _only_ set Created+Updated during a create, and Updated during an update. With this behavior it's possible and very likely for a resource to have Updated set with a nil Created. This is reasonable if we expect these to represent an exact timestamp and not a best guess.

I took a stab at a lifecycle test that goes through create → refresh → update steps. The timestamps _appear_ to propagate correctly but I feel uncertain. Is there some pathological case to consider that could accidentally set these to nil? Do reads matter? Failed operations? 🤔 

Folks using older CLI releases will of course reset these to nil -- that's unavoidable, and also a problem that lessens over time as people upgrade. We _might_ be able to detect this situation on the SaaS and generate some server-side timestamps, but I haven't looked into it yet.

Edit: one more thought -- we use `time.Now()` in the manifest's timestamp and it's worth confirming we want to keep this consistent and _not_ use UTC for these timestamps.

> Would it be better for lifecycle_handlers to return classes to instantiate instead of an instance?

Yes, I think so. Each lifecycle handler class will implement a uniform `.create()` method (or similar name), and Pants controls the creation of it.

> Is there any good place to put an integration test of lifecycle_handlers?

Yes, adding a test to `src/python/pants/init` would be awesome. I think you can maybe use `run_pants()`: https://www.pantsbuild.org/docs/rules-api-testing#approach-4-run_pants-integration-tests-for-pants. You would set up a `register.py` file in the test with your simple plugin, and set `--backend-packages` and `--pythonpath` to load it, similar to this:

Great catch!

1. C++98 does not define aspect of inf/nan values in text, but C99 does, and so, unlike `stod`, apparently stringstreams might not work in all platforms (specially MSVC) to parse `inf`, and `nan` values: https://www.boost.org/doc/libs/1_70_0/libs/math/doc/html/math_toolkit/fp_facets/facets_intro.html

2. The old implementation used `stod` (which is not locale-safe for our purposes): https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/string/basic_string/stof. See the old version of of this very function here: https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/commit/792c930305a818db1463911c2cbee92d462eaab9#diff-ddbdb79f73cc159561b86b759b0542fec75ca9f4869a7eac5790e8f9ebaf7ef4R382

3. We shouldn't stop parsing numbers when `fast_double_parser::parse_number` fails. There might be some numerical values that besides inf, or nan which are not parsed either. Hence we should keep the stringstream parsing for numerical values on the slow path instead of giving a fatal error.

There's just one thing that needs to be fixed around the use of the padding and oov tokens. AllenNLP `Vocabulary` objects make some assumptions about how padding is done that is not compatible with `transformers`. So the work-around is just make sure the transformer vocab namespace is considered a non padded namespace so that AllenNLP doesn't mess with it.

I also added a suggestion that we try to infer the OOV token from the tokenizer when we can.

And finally, could you add a test to `tests/data/vocabulary_test.py` that just makes sure we can save and load the vocab correctly. Something like this:

```python
class TestVocabularyFromPretrainedTransformer(AllenNlpTestCase):
    @pytest.mark.parametrize("model_name", ["bert-base-cased", "roberta-base"])
    def test_from_pretrained_transformer(self, model_name):
        namespace = "tokens"
        tokenizer = cached_transformers.get_tokenizer(model_name)

        vocab = Vocabulary.from_pretrained_transformer(model_name, namespace=namespace)
        assert vocab._token_to_index[namespace] == tokenizer.get_vocab()
        vocab.save_to_files(self.TEST_DIR / "vocab")

        vocab1 = Vocabulary.from_files(self.TEST_DIR / "vocab")
        assert vocab1._token_to_index[namespace] == tokenizer.get_vocab()
```

I use it to automatically report the project's code coverage, using the files produced by `coverage`.  It pretty much has to run in the same CI run.
It looks like there are official and non-official integrations (which even support combining the result of runs across versions), but this would mean substantial changes.

It looks like [the `coveralls` CLI tool that the project already uses can work within GitHub Actions](https://coveralls-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage/tox.html#github-actions), so I'd recommend using that.  You can update the `passenv` setting line 52 of `tox.ini`.

> I'm not really sure I understand the use of '#' characters

The `#` characters are just delimiters: `s#foo#bar#`. You can use any
single character; `/` is a common choice, but if there are lots of
slashes in the pattern, it can be nicer to use a different symbol. This
way, you don’t need to escape the slashes, and it’s easier to see which
parts of the expression are which.

One thing that I noticed (which we may address later in future PR), is that in `MULTI_INSTANCE` case we seem to always end up using external heap (basically the `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_EXTERNAL_HEAP_ENABLE` becomes "don't-care"). The question for me is why do we have this restriction and do we need it?
- When using internal heap, each `Instance` object will have its own `Utils::Heap` so technically we can have internal heap in multi-instance case.
- The challenge/reason may have been due to way the public OT API `otHeapCAlloc()` and `otHeapFree()` are defined. They don't have `otInstacne *aInstance` as parameter, so may not know which instance to use in the multi-instance case (but I guess we can either change them or even better remove them since they are marked to be deprecated).

Thoughts?

Cool! Nice approach that can further streamline the CORS UX. 🎉  

Was able to replicate with the following steps:

1. create a CloudFront distribution, as per our [docs](https://docs.localstack.cloud/user-guide/aws/cloudfront/):
```
$ awslocal s3 mb s3://test
$ echo 'Hello World' > /tmp/hello.txt
$ awslocal s3 cp /tmp/hello.txt s3://test/hello.txt --acl public-read

$ domain=$(awslocal cloudfront create-distribution --origin-domain-name test.s3.amazonaws.com | jq -r '.Distribution.DomainName')
$ echo $domain
ce6554a5.cloudfront.localhost.localstack.cloud
```

2. create a simple `index.html` file that connects to the enpoint:
```
<html>
<script>
fetch('http://ce6554a5.cloudfront.localhost.localstack.cloud:4566/hello.txt');
</script>
</html>
```

3. upload the file to a bucket and expose it as an S3 website:
```
awslocal s3 mb s3://test
awslocal s3 cp /tmp/index.html s3://test/index.html --content-type text/html
awslocal s3api put-bucket-website --bucket test --website-configuration 'ErrorDocument={Key=index.html},IndexDocument={Suffix=index.html}'
```

4. open http://test.s3-website.localhost.localstack.cloud:4566/index.html in the browser, observe the Network console (request to `ce6554a5.cloudfront.localhost.localstack.cloud:4566`)

Result:
* returns a `403` error on current master  🟥 
* returns 200 with the changes in this PR  ✅ 

---
To me, this achieves a decent balance between improving convenience (works out of the box without extra configs), while not sacrificing security (requests still only allowed from `*.localhost.localstack.cloud` pages in the browser). 👍 As usual with CORS, would be good to get a second pair of eyes on it, though.. 👀 /cc @dfangl @alexrashed @thrau

Cool! Nice approach that can further streamline the CORS UX. 🎉  

Was able to replicate with the following steps:

1. create a CloudFront distribution, as per our [docs](https://docs.localstack.cloud/user-guide/aws/cloudfront/):
```
$ awslocal s3 mb s3://test
$ echo 'Hello World' > /tmp/hello.txt
$ awslocal s3 cp /tmp/hello.txt s3://test/hello.txt --acl public-read

$ domain=$(awslocal cloudfront create-distribution --origin-domain-name test.s3.amazonaws.com | jq -r '.Distribution.DomainName')
$ echo $domain
ce6554a5.cloudfront.localhost.localstack.cloud
```

2. create a simple `index.html` file that connects to the enpoint:
```
<html>
<script>
fetch('http://ce6554a5.cloudfront.localhost.localstack.cloud:4566/hello.txt');
</script>
</html>
```

3. upload the file to a bucket and expose it as an S3 website:
```
awslocal s3 cp /tmp/index.html s3://test/index.html --content-type text/html
awslocal s3api put-bucket-website --bucket test --website-configuration 'ErrorDocument={Key=index.html},IndexDocument={Suffix=index.html}'
```

4. open http://test.s3-website.localhost.localstack.cloud:4566/index.html in the browser, observe the Network console (request to `ce6554a5.cloudfront.localhost.localstack.cloud:4566`)

Result:
* returns a `403` error on current master  🟥 
* returns 200 with the changes in this PR  ✅ 

---
To me, this achieves a decent balance between improving convenience (works out of the box without extra configs), while not sacrificing security (requests still only allowed from `*.localhost.localstack.cloud` pages in the browser). 👍 As usual with CORS or security-critical configurations, would be good to get a second pair of eyes on it, though.. 👀 /cc @dfangl @alexrashed @thrau
